text: We present a case of robot-assisted complex prostatic urethrocutaneous fistula excision.

Twenty-one-year-old male presented with complaints of passing urine and occasionally semen from the perianal wound, status post incision and drainage for perianal abscess which was done seven months ago.
A 0.5 cm external opening is visible at the four o'clock position in the perianal region.
During digital rectal examination, the surgeon palpates the prostate and notes minimal thickening on the left side, with no internal opening detected.
The MRI pelvis image shows a partially liquefied cystic structure communicating internally with the prostatic urethra and externally opening into the perianal region at the four o'clock position.
During cystoscopy, a diverticulum-like structure is observed arising from the left side of the prostatic urethra.
Patient was planned for robot-assisted urethrocutaneous fistula excision.
The surgeon begins posterior dissection between the bladder and rectum, carefully dividing the peritoneum to separate these anatomical structures.
A dilated cystic structure is observed replacing the prostate, visible in the surgical field.
On diagrammatic representation, we can see a complex cystic structure replacing the prostate with internal communication with the urethra and external communication with the perianal region.
During intraoperative cystoscopy, a large opening is visible at the seven o'clock position at the level of the prostatic urethra.
The surgeon visualizes a large cystic sac while negotiating the tract during the robot-assisted urethrocutaneous fistula excision.
The surgeon begins anterior dissection through the space of Retzius, attempting to access the cystic sac that could not be fully dissected from the posterior approach.
The surgeon approaches the cystic sac from the left side of the bladder, incising the endopelvic fascia and carefully dissecting the sac from surrounding anatomical structures.

The surgical field reveals a complex cystic structure replacing the prostate, with visible internal communication to the prostatic urethra and external communication to the perianal region, as correlated with MRI findings.
Stay sutures are applied to improve the visualization of the cystic sac, which is then divided.
During simultaneous cystoscopy, the surgeon identifies the fistulous opening into the urethra, visually confirming the connection between the cystic sac and the urethra.
The surgeon traces the remnant sac using stay sutures and proceeds to resect it, ensuring complete removal from the surrounding tissue.
Electrocautery should be used judiciously at this place to avoid injuring the neurovascular bundle.
A surgical team injects methylene blue through the external opening of the fistula, while another team begins dissection from the perianal region, highlighting the fistula tract.
The surgeon delineates the entire fistula tract using methylene blue, followed by complete excision of the tract through a combined abdominal perineal approach.
The surgeon repairs the urethra using Vicryl 3-0 sutures, carefully closing the identified fistulous opening.
The surgeon repairs the defect in the pelvis using StrataFix sutures, while the perineal wound is left open to heal by secondary intention.
Postoperatively, the patient was comfortable and discharged on day two.
Histopathology report was suggestive of fibrocollagenous tissue lined by non-keratinizing stratified squamous epithelium with chronic granulation tissue with no evidence of malignancy or dysplasia.
Follies was removed after three weeks and patient was able to void normally.
MRI pelvis was performed three months after surgery and showed edema at the site of resection which gradually resolved.
Patient was able to void normally, ejaculate and is sexually active.
Thank you.
